answers.explanation: Neural networks are computational models loosely inspired by the structure and function of the human brain, specifically by the way biological neurons communicate.
The loss function quantifies the difference between the network's prediction and the true label. Minimising it is the goal of training.
Backpropagation applies the chain rule of calculus to efficiently compute the gradient of the loss with respect to each weight layer by layer.

Sigmoid outputs saturate near 0 or 1, causing gradients to become near-zero during backpropagation (vanishing gradient). ReLU does not saturate for positive inputs, keeping gradients healthy in deep networks.

A large gap between training and test accuracy is the hallmark of overfitting. Dropout and L2 regularisation are standard remedies. Adding more layers would increase model capacity and likely worsen overfitting.

Early stopping halts training when validation loss stops improving. In the early phases of training a model learns genuine patterns; later it begins memorising noise. Stopping before that point implicitly limits model complexity without changing the architecture.
